core_intuitions: The stability-plasticity dilemma: continuing training must learn D_new without overwriting D_base, and LR height slides you between the two failure modes.

The three-phase re-warm / stable / re-decay LR schedule, and the counterintuitive fact that raising the LR causes a TRANSIENT loss spike on both old and new data that then recovers BELOW the starting loss after re-decay -- judge the run by where it settles, not the spike.

Replay: mixing a small fraction r of old-distribution data anchors the model; the single best diagnostic is the two-curve plot (new-domain loss falling steadily vs. base-domain loss rising slightly then plateauing), and larger r flattens the base-loss rise.

Function-preserving model growth: depth (zero the new residual block's output so it is the identity), width (Net2Net split-and-normalize), and dense->MoE upcycling (clone the dense MLP into every expert) all start the LARGER model computing the SAME function, then let CPT differentiate the new capacity.
Vocabulary transfer: initialize a new token's embedding as the mean of its old sub-token embeddings (and copy shared tokens unchanged) so the model already 'half knows' the new tokens.
Planning with a shifted power law L ~= L_inf + A/(D0+D)^alpha, where the D0 offset encodes the base model's head-start on the new domain -- fit from a short pilot to find the token knee.

existing.name: cpt-stability-plasticity-tradeoff
existing.issue: Renders correctly and legibly in both light and dark themes. Cleanly conveys the central dilemma: two bad operating points (too-hot / too-cold) on a downward-sloping naive frontier, with the re-warm+replay sweet spot lifted ABOVE the frontier. The 'replay lifts above frontier' annotation is exactly the right intuition. No clipping or overlap issues that impair reading.
existing.improvement: Optional only: in dark mode the 'GOOD CPT region' rounded box has slightly low contrast against the panel; a hair more fill opacity would help. Not required.

new_figures.name: cpt-rewarm-schedule-and-spike
new_figures.concept: The three-phase CPT LR schedule aligned above the loss trajectory it produces, showing the transient re-warm spike that recovers below baseline.
new_figures.rationale: This is the single most important knob in the chapter and its most counterintuitive claim: raising the LR makes loss JUMP UP on both old and new data before settling BELOW where it started. Practitioners abort good runs because the spike scares them. A static frame with the schedule and the loss curves vertically aligned makes 'don't panic at the spike; judge by the settled loss' click instantly -- text cannot land this the way aligned curves do.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Learning-Rate Re-Warming and Re-Decaying', after the '### The mechanics' three-phase list (before the schedule code).
new_figures.brief: A two-panel figure sharing one horizontal 'CPT steps' axis. TOP PANEL: the learning-rate schedule as a single line. It starts at a low floor (labeled 'resume at eta_min -- too cold to learn'), ramps LINEARLY up during a shaded 'Re-warm (T_w)' band to a peak marked with a dotted horizontal guide labeled 'eta_cpt ~ 10-30% of base peak'; holds FLAT across a wide 'Stable body' region; then curves down (cosine) through a shaded 'Re-decay (T_d)' band to a low floor labeled 'new floor'. Mark the original base peak as a faint dotted line ABOVE eta_cpt, labeled 'original pretraining peak (forgets most)', so the reader sees eta_cpt is a fraction of it. BOTTOM PANEL: two loss curves over the SAME x-axis, vertically aligned so events line up with the schedule above. Use the accent color for 'new-domain loss (D_new)' and a neutral/muted color for 'base-domain loss (D_base)'. Both curves: sit low-ish at step 0, then SPIKE UP sharply exactly under the re-warm band (a bump), the new-domain curve spiking then descending steadily to well below its start, the base-domain curve spiking then settling to only SLIGHTLY above its start. Draw a faint horizontal dashed reference line at each curve's step-0 value so the reader can SEE that the new-domain curve ends below its start line. Annotate the spike with a callout: 'transient spike -- stale Adam v_t + higher LR; recovers below baseline. Do NOT abort here.' Annotate the end of the new-domain curve: 'settled loss = judge the run here'. Keep everything inside the viewBox with generous margins; label the two curves with a small inline legend. Theme-safe tokens only (var(--v-accent), var(--ink-soft), var(--muted)); axes as illustrative sketches with NO numeric loss values -- only the eta_cpt/base-peak relationship and the phase names are labeled. Reading order: schedule (cause) on top, loss (effect) below, spike callout drawing the eye to the aligned bump.

new_figures.name: cpt-function-preserving-growth
new_figures.concept: The unifying trick behind depth growth, width growth, and dense-to-MoE upcycling: initialize the bigger architecture so it computes the SAME function, then let CPT differentiate.
new_figures.rationale: The whole second half of the chapter is three distinct-looking mechanics (zero a residual block, split-and-halve neurons, clone MLP into experts) that share ONE deep idea: preserve the function at init so the loss is unchanged at step 0, then wake up the new capacity gradually. Seeing all three side by side under one banner is far more foundational than any single one -- it turns three recipes into one principle. Completely unserved by the current single figure.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Model Growth & Weight Reuse', immediately after the intro paragraph (before '### Depth growth').
new_figures.brief: Three stacked (or side-by-side, whichever fits legibly) mini-panels under a shared banner reading 'Function-preserving init: bigger model computes the SAME function at step 0, then CPT differentiates'. Each panel shows a small BEFORE (trained small model) -> AFTER (grown model) transformation with a short caption of WHY the function is preserved. PANEL 1 DEPTH: a vertical residual stack of a few blocks x -> x + f(x); insert one NEW block (highlighted) whose output projection is drawn as a box labeled 'o_proj = 0, down_proj = 0'; annotate 'new block = identity (f(x)=0), input passes through untouched'. Show a tiny residual '+' merge with the new f(x) branch faded to zero. PANEL 2 WIDTH: a hidden layer of, say, 3 neurons; one neuron (highlighted) is DUPLICATED into two copies; label the incoming-copy 'clone row (+ tiny noise to break symmetry)' and the two OUTGOING weights each labeled 'w/2' with a caption 'divide outgoing weights by #copies -> summed output unchanged'. PANEL 3 DENSE->MoE: a single 'Dense MLP' box becomes an 'MoE layer' with E=4 experts each drawn as an IDENTICAL copy of the dense MLP (same fill), plus a small fresh 'Router (random init ~ uniform)' box feeding them; caption 'all experts identical + near-uniform router ~= original dense output; CPT differentiates experts'. Under all three, a shared bottom strip: a tiny loss-vs-steps sketch with the loss FLAT at the base value at step 0 (arrow: 'starts at base loss') then a gentle continued decline (arrow: 'CPT wakes up new capacity'). Use the accent color ONLY to highlight the newly-added / cloned element in each panel; everything reused is a neutral fill to signal 'same weights'. Small illustrative integers only (2-4 neurons/experts/blocks); no benchmark numbers. Theme-safe tokens. Ensure the three panels and captions do not overlap; keep clear left-to-right / top-to-bottom reading order with the unifying banner read first.

new_figures.name: cpt-replay-two-curve-diagnostic
new_figures.concept: The two-curve monitoring plot -- new-domain loss falling while base-domain loss rises then plateaus -- and how the replay ratio r flattens the forgetting curve.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter explicitly calls this 'your single best diagnostic' and gives the exact healthy/unhealthy signatures a practitioner should watch for. It also visualizes replay's core mechanism: a family of base-loss curves that flatten as r grows, because forgetting is driven by UNOPPOSED drift. This makes the replay-ratio knob concrete and gives readers a template for reading their own run's logs. Distinct from the re-warm figure (which is about the LR-induced spike); here the x-axis is the whole run and the focus is the base-vs-new divergence controlled by r.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Replay: Fighting Catastrophic Forgetting With Data', near the 'Practitioner tip: measure forgetting on a frozen held-out base set' admonition.
new_figures.brief: A single loss-vs-CPT-tokens plot with two thematic curve groups. NEW-DOMAIN loss (accent color, one solid curve): starts high, drops steadily and monotonically -- label 'new-domain loss: wants to fall (plasticity)'. BASE-DOMAIN loss: draw a FAMILY of three muted curves for three replay ratios, e.g. r=0 (no replay), r=0.05, r=0.30, each rising from the same step-0 point but rising LESS as r increases -- r=0 climbs steeply and keeps climbing (label 'r=0: monotonic climb = forgetting, unhealthy'), r=0.05 rises then PLATEAUS (label 'r~5%: small rise then plateau = healthy'), r=0.30 stays nearly flat (label 'r~30%: heavy anchor, diminishing extra benefit'). Use line-weight or dash style, not new hues, to distinguish the three replay curves so it stays theme-safe. Add a short caption box: 'Healthy run: new-domain loss falls; base-domain loss rises slightly then plateaus. If base loss climbs monotonically -> raise r or lower the re-warm peak.' Mark the step-0 base value with a faint dashed horizontal reference so the SMALL gap for r=0.05/0.30 vs the LARGE gap for r=0 is visible. Small illustrative values only, NO numeric loss or benchmark figures beyond the r labels (0, 0.05, 0.30 are the illustrative replay ratios the chapter itself uses). Legend distinguishing the accent new-domain curve from the muted base-domain family. Keep inside viewBox with room for the caption; reading order: title/axes, the two curve groups, then the diagnostic caption.

summary: The chapter is concept-rich but figure-poor: a single (good) stability-plasticity tradeoff diagram serves only the first of six core intuitions. Keep that figure (renders well in both themes, nails the 'replay lifts above the naive frontier' idea). Add three foundational figures: (1) PRIORITY 1 -- the re-warm/re-decay schedule aligned above its loss trajectory, making the counterintuitive TRANSIENT SPIKE-then-recover-below-baseline claim visible so readers don't abort good runs; (2) PRIORITY 1 -- a unifying 'function-preserving growth' infographic showing depth (identity block), width (net2wider split), and dense->MoE upcycling (clone MLP into experts) as ONE principle (same function at step 0, then differentiate); (3) PRIORITY 2 -- the replay two-curve diagnostic with a family of base-loss curves flattening as the replay ratio grows, the chapter's explicitly-named 'single best diagnostic'. Vocab-transfer mean-of-subtokens and the power-law planning offset are left to text/code as secondary.
